title: A catalogue on three levels
statement:
An empty editor. Build the catalogue from the target on the right.

Fourteen things, arranged on three levels. Two departments, two sections in each, and two items in each section.
constraints: Under Electronics: Phones (Samsung, Apple), Laptops (Gaming, Office)
Under Clothes: Men (Shirts, Trousers), Women (Dresses, Skirts)

why.empty: The editor is empty. There is no starter code here — you build the catalogue yourself.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed. At three levels this is the most common mistake — you lose track of which closing belongs to which opening.
why.no-heading: There is no heading, or it is empty.
why.few-items: There are not fourteen things. Two departments, four sections, eight items — that makes fourteen.
why.empty-items: There is a list item with no text in it. An empty box counts for nothing.
why.few-lists: There are not seven lists. One outer, two for the departments, four for the sections.
why.level-two: The second level is not nested. The sections sit next to the departments, not inside them.
why.level-three: The third level is not nested. The items are not under their section — they are somewhere else: either on the second level, or beside it. It looks indented, but the structure is flat.

level-three: Check where you close the of the section. Close it before the list of items and the items stay outside.
Three levels means a box in a box in a box. A department holds a section, a section holds an item. None of the three sits beside another.
Work from the inside out: build one complete section with its two items first, then put it into the department.
level-two: The department opens, a whole list of sections comes inside, and only then the department closes.
If the section comes after the of the department, it is a sibling, not a child.
not-closed: At three levels every <li> and every <ul> has a twin. The counts must match.
Use indentation. Each level two spaces further in — then you can see with your eyes where things end.
Close as soon as you open, then fill from the inside. That way you do not forget.
few-items: Count: two departments, four sections, eight items.
The departments and sections are list items too. They count.

solution.why:
At two levels you can still hold the structure in your head. At three you cannot — you lose track of which closing matches which opening, and you start gluing lists side by side.

The result looks almost right: everything is indented, everything has bullets. But half the things are not under their parent, they are next to it. The browser renders a mess that resembles a tree.
steps:
Do not start from the top. Build ONE complete section first:
  • Phones
    • Samsung
    • Apple
    • .
That is a finished brick. Build the second section the same way.
Put the two bricks in a list — that is the contents of one department.

Wrap it:
  • Electronics
      ...
    • . Now the department is a finished brick.
Finally wrap both departments in one outer list and put the heading above it.
solution.note: Deep structures are not built top down. They are built inside out — one finished brick, then another, then you put them together. That way you never hold more than one level in your head.
